speaker: interviewer
text: This is the fifth tape of an interview with Simon Stemer on November 13, 1995. You were telling us about the Russians coming in.

speaker: interviewee
text: We was waiting for the Russian to come in. It was taking time. Before the Russian coming, the German said the old people, the Polish, to go home and then not to leave. Polish people for the Russian, they come with the German. This was already July. In June, I have the last 15 ruble gold piece. This was in July. I give my boss to be paid up for another six week. If the war was taking longer, I don't know what they will be doing with me. All in meantime, when the German said like this, everybody have to leave, he bring us in this room a sack of wheat. Of course, you never know for how long they will be before the Russian will come in. We have water over there. We have a place for the wash room. We will eat wheat, and we will have at least wheat. This was not taking too long. This was until August 22 from April, and the Russian come in to Ludmir. The doctor met another woman that she-- they saw the Russian already walking in the street. I was thinking when I come out if I will see a Russian, I will fall to his leg and kiss his leg. He make me back to life. Is when I come out, is going in the street two young boys, maybe 14, 15 year old and two big machine guns. I was afraid to go close to him. As I went back to him, and we start to live. We went up. The bus was not in. All we took, it was apartments, is the whole family took an apartment, and we will live over there. When I was in the freedom, I went to meal over there what I work-- fish to give me something to eat. And later he gave me a little bit flour to have what to eat, and you go home. Going back from over there-- because we lived over there-- is going back from over there, is a guy for register for the military. He asked me my name. He read out my name, and he had tell me to come to the place to register for the military. I know I cannot walk on my legs, laying in this bunker so long. And I was thinking probably they will not take me to military. I'm not-- was a Jewish doctor what he was before working for the Russian. Even with the Russian, and he works in this [NON-ENGLISH] in the military. I ask him, Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], I cannot go to the military. He said, you will go. We will send you in a hospital for six weeks, and later you will go. I didn't want to go fight. Is I have a cousin from-- my sister-in-law has a cousin. He worked for the train. And when the Russian went, he went with them. And he come back. He took people for work by the train. He was trying to take in people and to send them to work. I go down to him, saying [NON-ENGLISH], they call it. And I tell him the story. He said, we have to send people what they can't find the German. You the guy-- whether you can fire against the German. We need you to the military. He was a communist, and he was working for the Russian government. I say, Chul, you know, I'm one left. Nobody in the family. Nobody. He took me in to work over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He asked me if I am alive, he have two suits. Who took his suits? If I was in the bunker and the police was taking, who knows. On a ride he took me to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Being there for work is I see a truck is coming with a Russian police, and he take the people to the military, the working people-- because I ran away. I ran away, and I come over there. I live with this doctor, [NON-ENGLISH] with this family. I live over there. They said if you will work again-- because I went and got a job in farmers bringing the milk to make the butter. I have that job. As I was thinking this guy what he was a Russian manager, he will protect me and need me for work and even I have protect. He went and he come back. No. He said, you have to go. They want me to come to work. This doctor, what he was-- he worked in the [NON-ENGLISH] for the Russian doctor. Is I ask him, he make for me a propose to go to Lublin. And I went to Lublin-- was already Jewish people. I met over there one cousin. In [NON-ENGLISH] he lived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh. [NON-ENGLISH] I think we have here. He lived here someplace. [NON-ENGLISH]. And I met him in Lublin. He took me, and I lived over there. Well, in Lublin-- and I don't have no money, nothing-- I was cleaned up. Only he give me some 50 zlotys, 100 zlotys. And I went in into the truck. Over there was a little truck. And I was doing buying from the soldiers and selling to the people. And I was sleeping over there by a guy, he was a tailor. Over there, my cousin lived upstairs. So there was no place. Putlepnik was his name. I was sleeping over there. And one day, I was on the truck, and I go to two Russian soldiers was, and I ask him if you have something for sale. They took me in to the commander. And they ask me. I told him I don't have what to eat. I ask him maybe if they have something to sell to me to have to eat something. They let me go. They don't care. Until long, they let me go. And I was on the truck. I was all right until the truck was the Polish government was in Lublin, and the military was going [NON-ENGLISH]. And they took later Sachsen. This was-- and I was liberated at 21. The 22nd of July 1944, and this was already in May 1945. It was liberated Sachsen. I went to Sachsen. On the train, when we went to Sachsen, I meet two young boys from Chelm, not far from our town, about 20 mile. We went together to Sachsen. And we come to Sachsen, they say that we was the first 11 people where they come into Sachsen, Polish people. We was three Jews and eight Pollocks. Over there, what we find that there are a room-- a lady. Downstairs was a little grocery store. Was empty shelves. I said, we open up a store with the two boys. We don't have nothing. I don't have nothing. They have little bit salad, bacon. They got about 10 kilos like this when we open up the store. Meantime, they start to bring in different things, the Russian. And the German were eating. We staying over there. And we did business. We did business. Meantime, there was good business to go to Sachsen-- from Sachsen to Berlin to bring in coffee and bacon. Is we arranged-- one was sitting in Berlin, and one was guy with a car back and forth. And I was in the store. This was until probably October or November. I got a telegram from my wife, from my girlfriend. My [NON-ENGLISH] they call it. She is back in [NON-ENGLISH] if I want to come. Is I left the store and I went, and I got married with her. And we come back to Sachsen with her. And we decided to go to Berlin. We sold the store, and we split. Is we got the $3,000 made in this time. Only this guy what they were sending to Berlin, he got already $20,000, he was doing business. And this guy what he was driving, he get $5,000. All I have $3,000, and I went with my wife to Berlin to [NON-ENGLISH]. And we was in the [NON-ENGLISH]. And I have a cousin in Chicago. My cousin, I wrote him a letter, and he send me papers to come to United State. As I arrive in United State, in June 21, 1947. My wife was pregnant, and we was caught. The counsel was not allowed to go when she pregnant. We wait until 1947. I come to United State. I come to United State. I come out from an orthodox family. I was thinking to be orthodox. I was looking to get a job by an orthodox place not to work on Saturday. I find a job in a department store by the name Mr. Robinson. The name is Robinson. He have a department store he have closed Saturday. Is I went to work for this Mr. Robinson. And when I was not working, I went over there-- I lived with my cousin in Chicago-- was a little park, was Wicker Park, was over there. A man, he walked the next street. And in the night, I saw him in the park. When I went to take the job by this Robinson, I know all he will pay me $30 a week or 35. And in Germany, it was possible to make $35 in half an hour. Only he told me-- he knocked me in my neck-- you will make a living already. When I go to this guy Robinson. All right. I work with this Robinson, and '48, my son got born. I said, Mr. Robinson, my family got bigger. Maybe you can give me a raise. He said, in three months later, I'll give you a raise. All I got little bit money from Germany, was not enough, $35 to live. My cousin give me the apartment. I don't pay. Only we got little bit money-- we used up little bit money. After being-- [COUGHS] After being 22 months work for Robinson, he start to give me already a raise. He comed up already to $70 a week. This was in '49. Meantime-- [COUGHS] Excuse me. My cousin have a candy store, and I live behind the candy store. He told me not too far from him is for sale a candy store, a good candy store. If he go in with his son-- he have a son Alex-- he can do over there-- he did about $600, $700 a week business. And if you go over there-- he said when he go in over there, he probably will do $1,000 or more because it was a better store like this, a bigger. All right. I bought the store. I have $2,000 left. In meantime, my cousin's wife-- his name was [NON-ENGLISH]-- I have vacation was in May or in June in '47. I went to look for a business. I come in in a grocery. The men want to give me the grocery for $8,000. And I don't have to pay him. I will pay him out when I go in the grocery. I come home, and I tell this to my cousin. My cousin's wife said, first time you have to be agree now. You have time when you want go in business, we always borrow you money. This what she told me. Is I quit the grocery. I don't take the grocery. Later my cousin told me about this candy store. I went to look, and I have $2,000. I want to buy the candy store with my cousin. He don't answer my to give him money. That man what he was selling the store is he said he will borrow me $2,500. Because I have already $4,500. He know the store, and he don't know me. Only he know he will pay out. He will work out, they will pay out. I was making $25 a week. Later, I need more money. Is one time when I come to work, I have over there a guy, a friend. [NON-ENGLISH] was his name. He asked me, Simon, look like you think something. I told him, I want to buy a candy store, and I don't have enough money. He said I have $1,000 I borrow to you. I said I give you something, I have a diamond-- he didn't want nothing. Now I still need $1,500. I went to my cousin. I said, Louie, help me out. I still need $1,500. He went with me to the bank, and he sign for me $1,500. When he comed home-- it's his friend was telling me-- his wife was firing so much-- you will lose the $1,500, and the other people will lose the-- She was crazy when I want to go into business, a greenhorn, after 22 months. Only I went in the store, and we moved then in the back. Was before the holidays. Is my lender-- he was the lender what I bought from the store. I come for rent before the holidays. I told him, Sam, you know, I will close for Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, the store. He said, Simon, don't do it here. The store was always open. And you will close, you will lose business. I told him, Saturday I have to work. I cannot make a living. He had my boss called when I bought the store, I call about not coming to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was not in the store. His son, I told him not to-- in the night he called me back. Simon, how come?

speaker: interviewee
text: He promised me-- the Mr. Robinson-- he will take me in for a partner. He promised me. And he called me up. I told you I will take you in for a partner. Oh, he don't take me in. He just promised. He will ask me one favor to be closed Saturday. I told him, Mr. Robinson, I bought it money. Saturday the best day. I have to pay the mortgage. If I will be closed, I will not have money to pay the mortgage, and I will not have a place to live. I will not be able to live. He said all right. He went out of the phone. OK. When I start my business, I told my landlord I will close. My landlord told me you take in $700 to $800 a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: I ask him, you guarantee for $800?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, if you will take in $700 for comic books, papers-- a candy store-- if you'll take in $700, you will make a living. I took in the first week $680. Only when it's come to Rosh Hashanah, he said you will spoil the business. I said, what will be will be. I will be closed. I closed Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, and the business start to be better. Business start to be better. In 1952, I bought an apartment building with 10 apartments. I have already money. I call up my cousin. I hear she was very mad-- a greenhorn, he is in business 20 years already in the candy store, and they still live. They still have just the candy store, and they have little bit money in the banks. And here, him just after two years, he bought already-- she was very mad. She was. Only when we went away and I bought this apartment building, and I worked out the store, it took in $2,000, $3,000 a week. I make bigger. I moved out from the store in my building, and I took an apartment. Took in a partner. The business was going good. Meantime, in '56, I bought a home. I moved out from my apartment. I moved in the home. In '59, I moved in in the home. We have two cars. My wife have a car. I have a car. I don't know if you know Chicago Lincolnwood suburb, good suburb. Lived over there. One day, we lived over there-- we moved in May, and this was in December-- Friday, my wife went-- Friday night. She went to pick up the boy from the barber, she got killed. In this time, I was feeling very bad. I wasn't be able to run the business. Meantime, my partner start to complain-- I'm stealing. You know. And he pushed me out from the business. All right. He pushed me out. I went in another business. I bought a supermarket with a friend, and we was over there about 10 years at the supermarket. Later, we sold the market, and I went in clothing business. When my wife had passed away, got killed, I married a woman. After a year, I married a woman with a boy. The marriage was not-- second marriage, if you give, you're a good husband. If you don't, you're not a good husband. To the kids, she was good. For me, she was not so good. Only I have to stay. She was for the kids good. I was just wondering the kids never said a word-- if momma was living, I was having this, I was having this. When my daughter graduate high school, she want to go away in a college. I told her, if you want stay in Chicago, I will buy you a car. I bought her a car, and she went to Northwestern. Later, my son graduate. No, he want to go away to Champaign. He was a year in Champaign. He don't like it over there. He come back, and he said, Daddy, if you buy me a car, I will stay. I bought him a car, and he start to go off racing. And later when he graduated, he went to Northwestern. Northwestern, when he finished, when he graduated, he applied for medical-- he applied to 10 schools. He was accepted in three-- in Illinois, in Northwestern, and in Iowa. In Iowa, they won't give him a scholarship for four years. In Northwestern, he can stay home or something. No. He said he want to go to Illinois.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alex, why you want to go to Illinois?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, if you go to a hospital-- in Illinois is a public hospital. Over there is no special doctors. In another hospital, every doctor have his patients. You don't have too much to say. In Illinois, you don't have no doctor. The doctors, they just come in for a while. They don't have the patients. He is over there more learning than this one. He went Illinois, and later he said he's being-- about a year, maybe eight months, he said, Daddy, I cannot stay home.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, it's not nice if I come home with a girl, and I will be in the room. I have to rent and give him an apartment. Stay in the dorm. No, he won't stay in the dorm. I rent and apartment. And later he got married. And my wife-- the second woman-- When I have later, I have a clothing store. Is when I retired in '79. I could retire four years later. My social security was four years earlier. Only the store was not too hard. The girl was working around. Said I will stay in the store another year, another year. I stay until '79, and I retired. And I was thinking now will be enjoyed. Is I went one time to Las Vegas with my wife and one time to San Francisco with my wife. And later, we come back. She went to the doctor, and they find out she have kidney troubles. She have to go to dialysis. She have to go to dialysis. We went over there. This was in summer. Only when it's go to winter will be hard to drive. Over there was far to drive about 15, 40 miles to the hospital. I went in the morning, and I wait over there until she got through. We decide we come to Florida. We come to Florida-- OK.
